skill.name: swiftui-liquid-glass
skill.description: Implement, review, and improve SwiftUI interfaces that use the iOS 26+ Liquid Glass API with native modifiers, containers, button styles, transitions, and fallbacks.
skill.summary: Build and review SwiftUI Liquid Glass UI using native iOS 26+ APIs.
skill.author: Dimillian

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, reconnaissance, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found these are false positives from SwiftUI documentation and Markdown code fences. No executable scripts, shell commands, credential access, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior were found in SKILL.md or references/liquid-glass.md. The only retained risk evidence is low informational network exposure from Apple documentation URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SwiftUI Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external_commands findings point to Markdown Swift examples and inline SwiftUI API names. They do not execute Ruby, shell commands, subprocesses, or user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged sections are fenced Swift snippets and SwiftUI modifiers such as glassEffect and buttonStyle. No shell metacharacters, command runner APIs, or executable scripts are present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Terms
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts match ordinary prose, SwiftUI terminology, and design words. No cryptographic algorithm, system probe, network scanner, or environment inspection is implemented.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found Liquid Glass documentation and UI guidance only. There is no code path for cryptography, host discovery, file inspection, or network probing.

low_findings.title: Apple Documentation Links Are Informational
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded Apple Developer documentation URLs in a references section. They are not fetched automatically and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references to developer.apple.com. No executable code or automation instructs the assistant to contact those URLs with user data.

content.value_statement: Adopting Liquid Glass can be hard because APIs, modifier order, and fallbacks must align. This skill guides SwiftUI implementation and review with native iOS 26+ patterns.
actual_capabilities: Identifies SwiftUI views that should use Liquid Glass effects.
Applies native glassEffect modifiers with appropriate shapes and interactivity.
Uses GlassEffectContainer for grouped glass elements and morphing behavior.
Reviews modifier order, button styles, and transition identifiers.
Adds iOS availability checks and non-glass fallback UI guidance.
limitations: Requires current Apple SDK knowledge for final API confirmation.
Does not compile or run Swift projects by itself.
Focuses on SwiftUI, not UIKit-only Liquid Glass implementations.
Cannot judge visual polish without screenshots or design context.

use_cases.title: Adopt Liquid Glass in a New Screen
use_cases.description: Plan and implement glass surfaces, buttons, and fallbacks for a new SwiftUI feature.

use_cases.title: Review Existing SwiftUI Glass Usage
use_cases.description: Check modifier order, container placement, interactivity, and iOS availability handling.

use_cases.title: Modernize Design System Components
use_cases.description: Update reusable chips, cards, and controls to use native Liquid Glass patterns consistently.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Glass
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add Liquid Glass to this SwiftUI view. Include iOS availability handling and a fallback.

prompt_templates.title: Review Glass Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SwiftUI feature for Liquid Glass correctness. Check modifier order, shapes, interactivity, and fallback behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Multiple Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor these SwiftUI components to use GlassEffectContainer, glass button styles, and consistent shapes where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Design Morphing Transitions
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Liquid Glass transition strategy for this SwiftUI flow. Use namespaces, glass identifiers, and animation guidance.

output_examples.input: A SwiftUI toolbar has several custom icon buttons and needs Liquid Glass styling.
output: Recommends grouping related controls in a GlassEffectContainer.
Suggests glass button styles for actions instead of custom blur backgrounds.
Notes where interactive glass is appropriate and where plain material should remain.

output_examples.input: An existing card view uses ultraThinMaterial and must support iOS 26 Liquid Glass.
output: Identifies the card as a candidate for native glassEffect.
Places glass styling after layout and appearance modifiers.
Keeps the existing material path as the fallback for earlier iOS versions.

output_examples.input: A feature expands from compact chips into a detail panel with animated state changes.
output: Recommends shared glass identifiers and a namespace for morphing.
Explains which elements should keep stable shapes during the transition.
Flags animation timing and spacing as design review points.

best_practices: Use native Liquid Glass APIs before custom blur or material effects.
Apply glass modifiers after layout and visual modifiers.
Add interactive glass only to controls that respond to user input.
anti_patterns: Do not wrap every surface in glass without a visual hierarchy reason.
Do not omit fallbacks for devices or OS versions without Liquid Glass.
Do not mix unrelated shapes and tints across a single control group.

faq.question: Does this skill write SwiftUI code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest SwiftUI changes and review Liquid Glass implementation patterns.

faq.question: Does it require iOS 26 or later?
faq.answer: The native Liquid Glass APIs are for iOS 26 and later. The skill also recommends fallback UI.

faq.question: Can it replace Apple documentation?
faq.answer: No. Use Apple documentation to confirm final API signatures and platform availability.

faq.question: Can it review visual design quality?
faq.answer: It can review patterns and consistency. Screenshots or design files improve visual feedback.

faq.question: Does it support UIKit?
faq.answer: It focuses on SwiftUI. UIKit-specific work may need separate guidance.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
